one_line_takeaway: Paralinguistic delivery style alone -- independent of textual content -- functions as a black-box adversarial lever against large audio-language models, boosting jailbreak ASR by roughly 10-20 percentage points over neutral audio/text and pushing Gemini 2.0 Flash to 98.26% ASR when combined with the AdvWave baseline.
key_finding: Stylized audio delivery consistently raises ASR over both text-only and plain/neutral-audio baselines across nearly all model-benchmark pairs. For the DeepInception paradigm (Table 1): GPT-4o Realtime ASR rose from 44.42% (text) to 57.88% (audio) on AdvBench, 40.00% to 66.67% on JailbreakBench, and 43.00% to 55.00% on MaliciousInstruct; Gemini 2.0 Flash rose from 86.15% to 88.65% (AdvBench), 84.00% to 88.33% (JailbreakBench), and 73.00% to 86.00% (MaliciousInstruct); Qwen2.5-Omni showed mixed/negative results (98.85% to 84.64% on AdvBench, 76.67% to 70.33% on JailbreakBench, 93.00% to 86.00% on MaliciousInstruct), attributed to decoding instability rather than stronger alignment. For the AdvWave paradigm (Table 2), the stylized variant beat the original AdvWave baseline on every model-benchmark pair, e.g., Gemini 2.0 Flash rose from 89.61% to 98.26% on AdvBench (the headline number cited in the abstract) and GPT-4o Realtime JailbreakBench rose from 87.00% to 96.33%. The delivery-style ablation (Table 4, DeepInception/AdvBench) shows neutral TTS conversion alone adds only 3-5% ASR versus text, while adding stylistic control (Authoritative Demand) adds a further 10-20 percentage points, isolating delivery style (not audio conversion per se) as the causal driver. A control experiment removing all jailbreak-style textual wrapping (Appendix A, tone variation on bare harmful instructions) found tone manipulation alone yields <10% ASR across conditions, showing delivery style amplifies existing jailbreak framings rather than acting as a jailbreak by itself. Human-recorded speech (20 AdvBench samples, Table 6) and an alternate TTS engine (Gemini-2.0-Flash TTS, 50 AdvBench samples, Table 7) reproduced the same qualitative ordering (stylized > plain audio > text), indicating the effect is not an artifact of one TTS system. Failure analysis on Qwen2.5-Omni (Section 5.3) found only 13.1% of failed audio-jailbreak attempts were genuine policy refusals; 86.9% were non-policy decoding failures (43.0% premature termination, 34.9% repetition loops, 9.1% overlap of both), attributed to the smaller model's weaker acoustic-embedding robustness rather than stronger safety alignment.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a black-box audio jailbreak against large audio-language models (LALMs) that manipulates paralinguistic delivery (prosody, tone, pacing, affect) rather than textual semantics or low-level signal perturbation. Grounded in psychological theories of persuasion and obedience (e.g., Guyer et al.'s work on vocal confidence, the CIVA model, Van Zant & Berger on vocal persuasion, Milgram's obedience studies), the authors design five heuristic TTS delivery styles -- Authoritative Demand, Affiliative Persuasion, Urgent Directive, Emotive Suggestion, and Social Bonding Appeal -- each implemented as a discrete control template fed to a TTS model (primarily GPT-4o Mini TTS) alongside the jailbreak text. The resulting stylized audio is used either to amplify an existing text-based jailbreak (DeepInception) or an existing audio-based jailbreak (AdvWave), and attack success rate (ASR) is measured via GPT-4o-as-judge against three target LALMs: GPT-4o Realtime, Gemini 2.0 Flash, and Qwen2.5-Omni-7B.
threat_model: Black-box adversary that can only submit audio waveforms to the target LALM (mapping M: A -> Y) and observe its textual output; no gradient or internal-state access. The attacker's objective is to find an audio input a* (or, more specifically, a speech-style vector s* rendered by a TTS model g_phi from a fixed textual prompt x, i.e., a = g_phi(x, s)) that maximizes the probability the model's output falls in a harmful/restricted response set R. This reframes the attack surface from audio-signal perturbation to delivery-style selection: the linguistic content x is held fixed while the paralinguistic style s is optimized/searched heuristically.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 8): (1) the attack is less effective on smaller LALMs (Qwen2.5-Omni), where added audio perturbation increases decoding instability rather than eliciting harmful content, producing unreadable/random outputs instead; (2) the five delivery styles are hand-crafted heuristics drawn from communication-psychology literature, with no systematic/automated optimization of vocal parameters; (3) the study is limited to English speech, so multilingual and cross-accent generalization is untested. Additional caveats evident from the paper's own data: the human-recorded-speech and alternate-TTS generalization checks use small samples (20 and 50 prompts respectively, vs. full benchmarks of 520/300/100), and ASR is measured entirely via a single LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o) rather than human annotation, which is a common but unverified proxy in this literature.

quantitative_results: DeepInception+audio vs text ASR, GPT-4o Realtime: AdvBench 44.42% -> 57.88%, JailbreakBench 40.00% -> 66.67%, MaliciousInstruct 43.00% -> 55.00%
DeepInception+audio vs text ASR, Gemini 2.0 Flash: AdvBench 86.15% -> 88.65%, JailbreakBench 84.00% -> 88.33%, MaliciousInstruct 73.00% -> 86.00%
DeepInception+audio vs text ASR, Qwen2.5-Omni: AdvBench 98.85% -> 84.64%, JailbreakBench 76.67% -> 70.33%, MaliciousInstruct 93.00% -> 86.00% (decreases, attributed to decoding instability)
AdvWave baseline vs stylized-audio-enhanced ASR, Gemini 2.0 Flash on AdvBench: 89.61% -> 98.26% (paper's headline number)
AdvWave baseline vs enhanced, GPT-4o Realtime JailbreakBench: 87.00% -> 96.33%

Delivery-style ablation (Table 4, DeepInception/AdvBench): Text vs Neutral Audio vs Stylized(Authoritative) -- GPT-4o Realtime 44.4% / 48.6% / 57.9%; Gemini 2.0 Flash 86.1% / 88.0% / 88.7%; Qwen2.5-Omni 98.8% / 80.3% / 84.6%
Tone-only control without jailbreak framing (Appendix A, Table 8), GPT-4o: Textual 5.0%, Plain Audio 6.0%, Authoritative Demand 6.0% (all <10%)

Human-recorded speech, 20 AdvBench samples (Table 6): GPT-4o Textual 50.0% / Plain Audio 45.0% / Authoritative Demand 55.0%; Gemini-2.0-Flash Textual 60.0% / Plain Audio 60.0% / Authoritative Demand 70.0%

Alternate TTS (Gemini-2.0-Flash TTS), 50 AdvBench samples (Table 7): GPT-4o Textual 46.0% / Plain Audio 44.0% / Authoritative Demand 54.0%; Gemini-2.0-Flash Textual 76.0% / Plain Audio 76.0% / Authoritative Demand 78.0%
Qwen2.5-Omni audio-jailbreak failure breakdown (Section 5.3): True Fail (genuine policy refusal) 13.1%; premature termination 43.0%; text-generation loop 34.9%; overlap of both 9.1%
baselines_compared: DeepInception (text-based recursive-narrative jailbreak, Li et al. 2024) as text baseline, vs. audio-rendered variant
AdvWave (audio-token-perturbation black-box jailbreak, Kang et al. 2024) as audio baseline, vs. style-augmented variant with 30 rounds of iterative refinement
Neutral/unstyled TTS audio as an ablation control against stylized audio
Human-recorded speech vs. synthetic TTS speech as a generalization check
GPT-4o Mini TTS vs. Gemini-2.0-Flash TTS as an ablation on the TTS engine used to generate attack audio
